perturbation.original_text: RECIPIENT agrees that for a period of five (5) years from the effective date of this Agreement, RECIPIENT will not disseminate the confidential information except to it’s employees who will be directly involved in the evaluation of the information, and furthermore RECIPIENT will use the same degree of care (but in no event less than reasonable care) to avoid disclosure or unauthorized use of such information as RECIPIENT employs with respect to confidential information of its own.
perturbation.changed_text: RECIPIENT agrees that for a period of one (1) year from the effective date of this Agreement, RECIPIENT will not disseminate the confidential information except to it’s employees who will be directly involved in the evaluation of the information, and furthermore RECIPIENT will use the same degree of care (but in no event less than reasonable care) to avoid disclosure or unauthorized use of such information as RECIPIENT employs with respect to confidential information of its own.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the confidentiality period to one year may be deemed unreasonable and insufficient to protect certain trade secrets under California law, particularly for sensitive technology like semiconductors and modular devices.
perturbation.law_explanation: CUTSA protects trade secrets for as long as they remain secret and provide a competitive advantage. Limiting the confidentiality period to one year, regardless of how long the information remains a trade secret, contradicts the intent of CUTSA to protect trade secrets for their useful life. The reasonableness of a protective period under CUTSA is a fact-specific inquiry, so one year may be too short given HALO's sensitive technology. For semiconductors and proprietary magnetics, a one-year protection period may be deemed manifestly insufficient to protect HALO's interests. An employer must take reasonable steps to maintain the secrecy of the information.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426. This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the confidentiality period from five years to one year. The Law Explanation states that limiting the confidentiality period to one year may contradict CUTSA's intent to protect trade secrets for their useful life, especially for sensitive technology. The Perturbation Explanation supports this, saying that a one-year period might be unreasonable under California law for certain trade secrets. Scraped Law Snippet 1 cites the California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA), providing the legal framework for trade secret protection, implying that reducing the protection period could be a violation if it's deemed insufficient to protect the trade secret. Because the original five-year period was substantially reduced to one year, and the explanations suggest this reduction could be insufficient to protect trade secrets under CUTSA, a contradiction exists.

perturbation.original_text: RECIPIENT recognizes that the remedy at law for any breach of its obligation under this Agreement will be inadequate, and hence, RECIPIENT agrees that HALO shall be entitled to equitable remedies, including injunction, in the event of breach by RECIPIENT.
perturbation.changed_text: RECIPIENT recognizes that the remedy at law for any breach of its obligation under this Agreement will be adequate, and hence, RECIPIENT agrees that HALO shall be entitled to monetary damages only in the event of breach by RECIPIENT.
perturbation.explanation: This modification removes HALO's ability to seek injunctive relief, potentially violating California law regarding trade secret misappropriation.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Code of Civil Procedure Section 526
perturbation.law_explanation: California Code of Civil Procedure Section 526 allows for injunctive relief to prevent the breach of an obligation. By stating that HALO is only entitled to monetary damages, the contract prohibits HALO from seeking an injunction to stop the misappropriation of trade secrets, which is a remedy commonly granted in such cases and permitted by law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CCP 526 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Code of Civil Procedure - CCP PART 2. OF CIVIL ACTIONS [307 - 1062.34] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 7. OTHER PROVISIONAL REMEDIES IN CIVIL ACTIONS [501 - 574] ( Heading of Title 7 added by Stats. 1974, Ch. 1516. ) CHAPTER 3. Injunction [525 - 534] ( Chapter 3 enacted 1872. ) 526. (a) An injunction may be granted in the following cases: (1) When it appears by the complaint that the plaintiff is entitled to the relief demanded, and the relief, or any part thereof, consists in restraining the commission or continuance of the act complained of, either for a limited period or perpetually. (2) When it appears by the complaint or affidavits that the commission or continuance of some act during the litigation would produce waste, or great or irreparable injury, to a party to the action. (3) When it appears, during the litigation, that a party to the action is doing, or threatens, or is about to do, or is procuring or suffering to be done, some act in violation of the rights of another party to the action respecting the subject of the action, and tending to render the judgment ineffectual. (4) When pecuniary compensation would not afford adequate relief. (5) Where it would be extremely difficult to ascertain the amount of compensation which would afford adequate relief. (6) Where the
perturbation.justification: The modification explicitly contradicts California Code of Civil Procedure Section 526, which allows for injunctive relief. The original text acknowledges the inadequacy of legal remedies and allows for equitable remedies, including injunctions. The changed text limits HALO to monetary damages, directly prohibiting them from seeking an injunction, which is a remedy permitted by CCP 526(a)(1) when 'the plaintiff is entitled to the relief demanded, and the relief, or any part thereof, consists in restraining the commission or continuance of the act complained of'. The modified text therefore removes a remedy that the law allows, creating a legal contradiction as it potentially limits HALO's ability to prevent trade secret misappropriation through injunctive relief.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws (excluding the laws of conflict of laws) of the State of Califor nia.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws (excluding the laws of conflict of laws) of the State of Delaware.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law to Delaware could potentially violate California's strong public policy interest in protecting its trade secrets, especially if the recipient is based and operates primarily in California, undermining California's jurisdiction over the matter.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600
perturbation.law_explanation: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600 invalidates contracts that restrain a person from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business. While NDAs are generally permissible, a choice of law provision favoring Delaware law (which might be more lenient on restrictive covenants) could be challenged if it effectively prevents a California-based employee from working in their field after leaving the company. California courts are hesitant to enforce out-of-state laws that conflict with this fundamental policy. Applying Delaware law could potentially violate California's strong public policy against restraints of trade.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, BPC 16600 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Business and Professions Code - BPC DIVISION 7. GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 1. Contracts in Restraint of Trade [16600 - 16607] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) 16600. (a) Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. (b) (1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937, to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context, or any noncompete clause in an employment contract, no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter. (2) This subdivision does not constitute a change in, but is declaratory of, existing law. (c) This section shall not be limited to contracts where the person being restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is a party to the contract. (Amended by Stats. 2023, Ch. 828, Sec. 1. (AB 1076) Effective January 1, 2024.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text changes the governing law from California to Delaware. The Law Explanation states that this shift could conflict with California's public policy against restraints of trade, particularly in relation to NDAs and non-compete agreements. Scraped Law Snippet 1, specifically BPC 16600(a), explicitly voids contracts that restrain someone from engaging in a lawful profession in California. If Delaware law is more lenient on restrictive covenants and is applied to a California-based employee, it could violate this section. This creates a legal contradiction because the choice of law could undermine California's statutory protections for its workers, making the altered text potentially unenforceable in California.

perturbation.original_text: All information furnished to RECIPIENT by HALO shall, unless otherwise specified in writing by HALO, remain the property of HALO; and the written information, and any copies thereof, shall be promptly returned to HALO upon its written or destroyed by RECIPIENT at HALO’s option.
perturbation.changed_text: All information furnished to RECIPIENT by HALO shall, unless otherwise specified in writing by HALO, become the property of RECIPIENT; and the written information, and any copies thereof, shall be destroyed by RECIPIENT after the evaluation.
perturbation.explanation: Stating that the confidential information becomes the property of the recipient may violate California's trade secret laws if it involves misappropriation. HALO is located in California and the agreement involves HALO proprietary information.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA) - Misappropriation
perturbation.law_explanation: CUTSA defines misappropriation as acquisition of a trade secret by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means. By stating that the information becomes the recipient's property, it implies a transfer of ownership of potential trade secrets which could constitute misappropriation, especially if the recipient uses improper means to acquire or use the information.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3426.1 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The modified text states that confidential information furnished by HALO becomes the recipient's property. This directly contradicts California's Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA) as it could constitute misappropriation, particularly if the recipient uses improper means to acquire or use the information. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines "misappropriation" as the acquisition of a trade secret by someone who knows or has reason to know it was acquired by improper means (CIV 3426.1). By transferring ownership, the contract potentially enables the recipient to misappropriate HALO's trade secrets, making the changed text a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: HALO Agrees to transmit to RECIPIENT confidential information for evaluation purposes only.
perturbation.changed_text: HALO Agrees to transmit to RECIPIENT confidential information for any purpose.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the 'evaluation purposes only' restriction might violate California law if the recipient uses the information for purposes outside the scope of the agreement, potentially constituting trade secret misappropriation.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA) - Misappropriation
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code Section 3426.1(b)(2) defines 'misappropriation' as disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who knew or had reason to know that his knowledge of the trade secret was (i) derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it, (ii) acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use, or (iii) derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use. By allowing the recipient to use the information for any purpose, it removes the limitation on use that would otherwise exist. This would cause misappropriation under CUTSA if, for example, the RECIPIENT uses that information for its own profit.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The modified text removes the 'evaluation purposes only' restriction, allowing the recipient to use the confidential information for any purpose. This directly contradicts California Civil Code Section 3426.1(b)(2), as outlined in the Law Explanation, because it eliminates the limitation on use that would otherwise exist, potentially leading to misappropriation. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines 'misappropriation' as 'Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who...knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was...Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use.' By removing the purpose limitation, the changed text allows for uses outside the original agreement, potentially breaching the duty to limit its use, and thus creates a contradiction.
